name: Indium
name_origin: From the Latin 'indicum' (indigo), referring to the bright indigo blue line in its atomic emission spectrum.

discovery.discoverer: Ferdinand Reich and Hieronymous Theodor Richter

occurrence.abundance_summary: Very rare in Earth's crust (about 0.05 ppm); not found free in nature, occurs primarily as a trace constituent in zinc and lead-zinc sulfide ores such as sphalerite. It is recovered as a byproduct of zinc refining.

compounds.name: Indium(III) oxide
compounds.summary: A pale yellow solid; a key precursor to indium tin oxide (ITO) used in transparent conductive coatings.

compounds.name: Indium(III) chloride
compounds.summary: A white, hygroscopic solid used as a Lewis acid catalyst in organic synthesis.

compounds.name: Indium phosphide
compounds.summary: A III-V semiconductor used in high-frequency electronics, photonics, and laser diodes.

compounds.name: Indium arsenide
compounds.summary: A narrow band-gap semiconductor used in infrared detectors and high-mobility transistors.

compounds.name: Indium(III) hydroxide
compounds.summary: A white amphoteric solid that is an intermediate in indium ore processing.

uses: Indium tin oxide (ITO) transparent conductive coatings for LCD displays, touchscreens, and solar cells
Low-melting fusible alloys (such as Field's metal) for safety devices and specialty solders
Compound semiconductors (InP, InGaAs) for fiber-optic communications and photodetectors
Cold-welding and vacuum seals exploiting indium's softness and ductility
Coatings on bearings to reduce wear and corrosion in high-performance engines
reactivity_summary: Indium is a soft, silvery, ductile post-transition metal that is stable in dry air at room temperature but tarnishes slowly to form a thin oxide layer. It burns at higher temperatures to give In2O3 with a violet flame, dissolves in mineral acids, and is not attacked by alkalis. Its most stable oxidation state is +3, although +1 compounds are also known.
isotopes_summary: Indium has two naturally occurring isotopes: indium-113 (stable, 4.28% abundance) and indium-115 (95.72% abundance), which is weakly radioactive with a half-life of 4.41 x 10^14 years. About 40 synthetic radioisotopes are known; indium-111 is widely used in nuclear medicine for diagnostic imaging.
